retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
heading_path: How do my documents become translated?
retrieved.text: We only accept documents that were issued in German or English. If your documents were issued in a different language, a translation is required. Please regard the following terms: - If full diplomatic certification is required, your documents have to be legalised first. Only then they may be translated including all stamps, seals, and certification notes. - An apostille must also be translated. This means that the apostille has to be affixed to the original document first. Afterwards it can be translated. - The documents must be translated by official sworn and court certified translators. - The translation must be inseparably attached to the original document. Do not detach the documents (not even when scanning them!). Else the translation will be void. # FAQ / - Which documents require diplomatic certification? We require diplomatic certification of all documents that attest your prior education. - Bachelor/diploma programmes: All pages of your secondary school leaving certificate and, if applicable, transcripts of records/diplomas (if you have studied at a university before). - Master programmes: Your higher education degree certificate, relevant transcripts of records and/or the diploma supplement. - My documents have not been diplomatically certified yet. retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
retrieved.text:
You wish to apply for admission and your documents were issued in a non-EU/EEA country? Please have them translated after they were diplomatically certified (i.e. legalised) first.

Your documents were issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland? Such documents need only [be translated](#c1122153), while legalisation is not required.

There are four possibilities:

- [Full diplomatic certification](#c1121827)
- [Apostille](#c1121828)
- [Diplomatic certification is not required](#c1121829)
- [Diplomatic certification is temporarily deferred](#c1121830)

retrieved.title: Additional bachelor/diploma programme
heading_path: FAQ
retrieved.text:
/

- My secondary-school leaving certificate is from an EU/EEA country, but I have citizenship of a non-EU/EEA country. How can I be admitted to an additional degree programme?

  Submit your application for admission within the application period for non-EU/EEA citizens. Follow the steps for changing your degree programme with secondary-school leaving certificate and citizenship from an EU/EEA country. In this way, your admission to an additional degree programme can be conducted earlier and faster – and you can start your new degree programme in a less stressful way.

  You can prove that you belong to a group of persons according to the [Decree on Groups of Persons](/en/non-eueea-nationals-with-equivalent-status/)? If this is the case, you can also submit your application within the application period for EU/EEA citizens. Submit the proof of belonging to a group of persons. The admission to the additional degree programme will take longer, as your application must be processed individually, and you have to wait for your admission letter. With this letter you will also receive the info you need to complete the admission to the additional degree programme.

- My secondary-school leaving certificate is from an EU/EEA country, but I have citizenship of a non-EU/EEA country. How can I change my degree programme

  Submit your application for admission within the application period for non-EU/EEA citizens. Follow the steps for changing your degree programme with secondary-school leaving certificate and citizenship from an EU/EEA country. In this way, your change of degree programme can be conducted earlier and faster – and you can start your new degree programme in a less stressful way.

  You can prove that you belong to a group of persons according to the [Decree on Groups of Persons](/en/non-eueea-nationals-with-equivalent-status/)? If this is the case, you can also submit your application within the application period for EU/EEA citizens. Submit the proof of belonging to a group of persons. The change of degree programme will take longer, as your application must be processed individually, and you have to wait for your admission letter. With this letter you will also receive the info you need to complete the change of degree programme.

heading_path: FAQ
retrieved.text: / - I had English lessons in school. Is this a proof of English language proficiency? Having had English lessons in school is not sufficient as proof of language proficiency. A secondary-school leaving certificate with successful graduation exam in the subject English from a school in an EU/EEA country, Switzerland, the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, the USA, New Zealand or Canada is an accepted proof, if the level of English proficiency is noted as B2 or higher. If the level is not clearly evident in the certificate, a confirmation by the school administration may be demanded. - I have completed my studies in a non-EU/EEA country. retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
retrieved.text:
1. Inform yourself in the issuing country which national public authority may issue an apostille, and if there are any requirements.
2. Have an apostille added to your documents.
   The apostille can be added on the document itself, on an attached page, or by means of an electronic apostille. Do not detach the apostille! The apostille must attest any signature(s) by the issuing educational institution (directorate, rectorate, or deanship) that the document bears. The signature of the notary or translation agency may not be attested.
3. Have your documents translated.
   Your [documents as well as the apostille has to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
4. Apply for admission to a study programme.
   Scan all pages of your document, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Documents issued in certain countries do not need to be diplomatically certified. However, we require an [official translation in German or English](#c1122153), if the documents were not issued in either of these languages.

Documents issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland are exempt from all legalisation requirements.

retrieved.title: Proof of English language proficiency
heading_path: Recognised proofs of English language proficiency at level B2
Proofs B2
retrieved.text:
- Secondary-school leaving certificate with successful graduation exam in the subject English. The following conditions apply:
  - The secondary-school leaving certificate was completed at a school of an EU/EEA country, Switzerland, the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, the USA, Australia, New Zealand or Canada.
  - If the level of English proficiency is not clearly evident in the certificate, a confirmation by the school administration may be demanded.
  - The Austrian secondary-school leaving certificate (Matura) of the Corona year 2021 is equal to other secondary-school leaving certificates.
- "IB diploma" according to the regulations of the "International Baccalaureate Organization" with the subject English.
- Certificate of the supplementary examination in the subject English from the University Preparation Programme of an Austrian University.
- "Studienberechtigungszeugnis" from an Austrian university with a successfully completed examination in the subject English.
- Degree diploma and transcript of records/diploma supplement, which fulfills all of the following conditions:
  - full-time degree programme of at least two years and
  - taught entirely in English and
  - at a university in an EU/EEA country, Switzerland, the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, the USA, Australia, New Zealand or Canada.

These proofs are valid for an unlimited time.

Any of the above mentioned proofs at a higher level than B2, can also be accepted as proof of English language proficiency at level B2 ([see FAQ](#c805025)).

General requirements for the application for admission to bachelor/diploma programmes
retrieved.text:
- scan of your valid your passport or personal ID card (front and back!) as JPEG or PNG file
- all pages (!) of your original secondary-school leaving certificate
  - including the required [legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) according to the issuing country
  - plus sworn translation (if not issued in German or English)
- [list of subjects](/en/admission/bachelordiploma-programmes/list-of-subjects/) in its original version
  - including the required [legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) according to the issuing country
  - together with a sworn translation (if not issued in German or English)
- if you have already studied: transcripts/diplomas in their original version
  - including the required [legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) according to the issuing country
  - plus sworn translation (if not issued in German or English)
- for German and multilingual programmes: [proof of German language proficiency](/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/) at level A2 or higher
- for the bachelor programmes in International Legal Studies and Mathematical Foundations of Data Science: [proof of English language proficiency](/en/admission/english-language-proficiency/) at level B2 or higher

You have to submit the application for admission within the [application period](/en/admission/application-and-admission-periods/). The application period depends on your citizenship. Please note: [degree programmes with entrance exam procedure](/en/entrance-exam/) have a special application period.

retrieved.title: Competence check at VFS Global
heading_path: Step 1: Inform yourself about the admission requirements
retrieved.text:
Your documents have to be legalised by [apostille](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/#c1121828) and must be translated into either German or English afterwards.

Your steps in the admission procedure depend on the degree programme of your choice.

/

- Bachelor/diploma programme

  If your secondary-school leaving certificate (12 years of education) is from India and you have achieved at least 85% of the maximum possible marks, your application can be processed. Each application must be reviewed individually.

  [Detailed information about admission to a bachelor or diploma programme](/en/admission-procedure/bachelordiploma-programmes/)
- Master programme

  Your application can be processed if:

  - You have completed a bachelor programme with a stipulated duration of at least three years (full-time) at a recognised university AND
  - your university is classified as Section 1 or 2 (according to ENIC NARIC AUSTRIA Credential Evaluation) AND
  - you have achieved at least 60% of the maximum points available in your studies.

  OR, if:

  - You have completed a bachelor programme with a stipulated duration of at least three years (full-time) at a recognised university AND
  - you have completed your bachelor programme at a state university, which is classified as Section 3 (according to ENIC NARIC AUSTRIA Credential Evaluation) AND
  - you have achieved at least 80% of the maximum points available in your studies.

  Each application must be reviewed individually.

  [Detailed information about admission to master programmes](/en/admission-procedure/master-programmes/)

1. Inform yourself in the issuing country which national public authority may issue an apostille, and if there are any requirements.
2. Have an apostille added to your documents.
   The apostille can be added on the document itself, on an attached page, or by means of an electronic apostille. Do not detach the apostille! 4. Apply for admission to a study programme.
   Scan all pages of your document, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Documents issued in certain countries do not need to be diplomatically certified. However, we require an [official translation in German or English](#c1122153), if the documents were not issued in either of these languages.

Documents issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland are exempt from all legalisation requirements.

Your documents were issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland? Such documents need only [be translated](#c1122153), while legalisation is not required.

There are four possibilities:

- [Full diplomatic certification](#c1121827)
- [Apostille](#c1121828)
- [Diplomatic certification is not required](#c1121829)
- [Diplomatic certification is temporarily deferred](#c1121830)

selected.title: Additional bachelor/diploma programme
heading_path: FAQ
selected.text:
/

- My secondary-school leaving certificate is from an EU/EEA country, but I have citizenship of a non-EU/EEA country. How can I be admitted to an additional degree programme?

  Submit your application for admission within the application period for non-EU/EEA citizens. Follow the steps for changing your degree programme with secondary-school leaving certificate and citizenship from an EU/EEA country. In this way, your admission to an additional degree programme can be conducted earlier and faster – and you can start your new degree programme in a less stressful way.

  You can prove that you belong to a group of persons according to the [Decree on Groups of Persons](/en/non-eueea-nationals-with-equivalent-status/)? If this is the case, you can also submit your application within the application period for EU/EEA citizens. Submit the proof of belonging to a group of persons. The admission to the additional degree programme will take longer, as your application must be processed individually, and you have to wait for your admission letter. With this letter you will also receive the info you need to complete the admission to the additional degree programme.

heading_path: L
selected.text: Legalisation Documents required for admission must be legalised if they were issued in a non-EU/EEA country. Depending on the country of issuance, two types of legalisation are possible: an apostille or a full legalisation. Related Links: - [Information regarding the apostille](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) - [Information about full legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) Related entries: - [Apostille](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31900&cHash=b73f3e20226f1cd8a29d5f74338e38cf) - [Full legalisation](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31996&cHash=b2d27c84616f8a39b0d4f3ad8770c2f8) - List of subjects If your secondary-school leaving certificate is from a non-EU/EEA country, you need a list of subjects to apply for admission to a bachelor/diploma programme. In many countries, the list of subjects is part of the school-leaving certificate. It shows how many hours of a subject you have completed in the course of your schooling. Related links: - [List of subjects](/en/admission/bachelordiploma-programmes/list-of-subjects/) - Livestreaming Teachers who hold courses in u:stream lecture halls can offer their courses as a live stream. There are two types of live streams: a web stream or a room2room stream. Audio and video recordings can be made available to the students on the e-learning platform (=Moodle). Related Links: - [Recording courses – u:stream](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/ustream/) - [E-learning platform Moodle](https://moodle.univie.ac.at) Related entries: - [Moodle](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31195&cHash=f09a4dd9dabecf19aab1edecd36ac050)

heading_path: How do my documents become translated?
selected.text: diplomatically certified yet. Can I still apply for admission? No, as you may only apply once all documents have been diplomatically certified and translated, as required. If your application is incomplete, it may prolong its lead time. - For how long is the diplomatic certification valid? A diplomatic certification is valid indefinitely. - I am a non-EU/EEA citizen, but I previously studied in an EU/EEA country/Switzerland or graduated from school there. Do my documents require diplomatic certification? Your citizenship has no influence on whether your documents require diplomatic certification. Am I required to have an additional translation made? No, the University of Vienna accepts documents issued in two languages, if one of the languages is German or English.

[1] Legalisation and translation — How do my documents become translated?
We only accept documents that were issued in German or English. If your documents were issued in a different language, a translation is required. Please regard the following terms: - If full diplomatic certification is required, your documents have to be legalised first. Only then they may be translated including all stamps, seals, and certification notes. - An apostille must also be translated. This means that the apostille has to be affixed to the original document first. Afterwards it can be translated. - The documents must be translated by official sworn and court certified translators. - The translation must be inseparably attached to the original document. Do not detach the documents (not even when scanning them!). Else the translation will be void. # FAQ / - Which documents require diplomatic certification? We require diplomatic certification of all documents that attest your prior education. - Bachelor/diploma programmes: All pages of your secondary school leaving certificate and, if applicable, transcripts of records/diplomas (if you have studied at a university before). - Master programmes: Your higher education degree certificate, relevant transcripts of records and/or the diploma supplement. - My documents have not been diplomatically certified yet. Can I still apply for admission? No, as you may only apply once all documents have been diplomatically certified and translated, as required. If your application is incomplete, it may prolong its lead time. - For how
